title: Environment Variables
description: Learn how to use variables for dynamic command customization

sections.title: What Are Variables?
sections.content:
Variables are named placeholders that store values you can use in aliases and triggers. They make your automation more flexible and reusable.

MUDPuppy has two types of variables:
- **User variables ($)** - Values you define and can change
- **System variables (%)** - Automatic values from the client

sections.title: User Variable Syntax
sections.content:
Use the $ or ${variable_name} syntax to reference user variables:

| Variable | Usage | Example Result |
|----------|-------|----------------|
| target | cast ${target} | cast goblin |
| heal | drink ${heal} | drink potion |
| attack | kill ${attack} | kill dragon |

The ${...} is replaced with the variable's value when the command is sent.

You can use either $name or ${name} - they mean the same thing.

sections.title: System Variable Syntax
sections.content:
System variables provide automatic client information:

**Numeric form (%1, %2, etc.):**
These are session-scoped arguments:
- %1 - First argument after command
- %2 - Second argument
- And so on...

These are automatically populated during alias/trigger expansion and cleared when you disconnect.

**Named form (%TIME, %CHARACTER, etc.):**
These are read-only client values:
| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| %TIME | Current time (HH:MM:SS) |
| %DATE | Current date (YYYY-MM-DD) |
| %CHARACTER | Your character name |
| %SERVER | MUD server address |
| %SESSIONID | Session identifier |
| %HOST | Host address |
| %PORT | Port number |

sections.title: Creating a User Variable
sections.content:
To create a user variable:

1. Go to Connection Settings
2. Navigate to Environment Variables
3. Click 'Add Variable'
4. Enter a name (e.g., 'target')
5. Enter the value (e.g., 'goblin')
6. Click Save

Your variable is available immediately in aliases and triggers.

You can also create variables using the #SET command:
#SET target=dragon

sections.title: Using Variables in Aliases
sections.content:
Variables work seamlessly with aliases:

Example 1 - Combat alias:
- Pattern: 'k'
- Type: prefix
- Replacement: kill ${target}

When you type 'k goblin', it sends 'kill goblin'.
If you change target='dragon', it sends 'kill dragon' automatically.

Example 2 - Healer alias:
- Pattern: 'h'
- Type: exact
- Replacement: drink ${heal}

Type 'h' to drink your configured healing item.

sections.title: Using Variables in Triggers
sections.content:
Variables can also be used in trigger commands:

Example:
- Match: 'You are hungry'
- Action: 'eat ${food}'

When triggered, it sends 'eat bread' (or whatever food is set to).

sections.title: Variable Persistence
sections.content:
User variables are saved with your connection profile:

- They persist across sessions
- They're specific to each saved connection
- Changing a variable affects all aliases/triggers that use it

System variables (%1, %2, etc.) are session-scoped:
- They exist only for your current session
- They're cleared when you disconnect
- They're automatically set during alias/trigger expansion

sections.title: Example Workflow
sections.content:
Here's a practical example:

1. Create variable: target = goblin
2. Create variable: heal = potion
3. Create alias: pattern='k', replacement='kill ${target}'
4. Create alias: pattern='dr', replacement='drink ${heal}'

Now:
- Type 'k' to kill goblins
- Type 'dr' to drink potions

When you want to fight dragons instead:
- Change target = dragon
- Now 'k' kills dragons automatically!

Use the 'Add Example' button for ready-to-use variable templates.

sections.title: Undefined Variables
sections.content:
If you reference a variable that doesn't exist, it passes through literally:

- ${undefined} → ${undefined}
- $nonexistent → $nonexistent

This lets MUD commands work normally if the variable isn't set.
